Subplot B (top-right): Surface mesh for d1 operator magnitude |D1(u,v)| = |sin(2 * pi * u)|, flat along v
Subplot C (bottom-left): Surface mesh for Roberts Cross magnitude |H_R(u,v)| = |sin(pi * (u + v))| (diagonal ridge)
Subplot D (bottom-right): Surface mesh for Sobel-Feldman magnitude |S(u,v)| = |sin(2 * pi * u)| * (2 + 2 * cos(2 * pi * v)) / 4 (localized bandpass lobes)
Ultra-thin 3D line-arrow coordinate axes for each subplot, styled to match clean textbook pen strokes

Text labels for 'v' placed cleanly at the outer tip of the depth axes (+z)

Distinct vertical labels: '|D_0(u,v)|' for (a), '|D_1(u,v)|' for (b), '|H(u,v)|' for (c), and '|Sobel_x(u,v)|' for (d), offset upward and outward from the vertical axis tip (+y) to prevent any mutual overlapping
Synchronized OrbitControls that rotate all 4 plots in perfect unison

interactions.label: Layout
interactions.teaches: Switches between comparing all four operators side-by-side or zooming in on a single operator's detailed frequency response

interactions.label: Color map
interactions.teaches: Toggles between a vibrant height-based warm-to-cool colormap and a clean, high-contrast monochrome grid that highlights surface curvature

demo_steps.title: Comparing all four operators
demo_steps.narration: Observe all four derivative operators side-by-side in 3D. While they all approximate spatial derivatives, their frequency responses differ dramatically. Drag to rotate the grid in unison and compare their 3D profiles from any angle.

demo_steps.title: d0: High-frequency noise risk
demo_steps.narration: Zooming into the d0 operator [1, -1]. Because its response rises monotonically to a peak at the highest frequencies (u = ±0.5) and is flat along v, it acts as a pure high-pass filter that is highly sensitive to pixel-level noise.

demo_steps.title: d1: Central difference roll-off
demo_steps.narration: The central difference d1 [1, 0, -1] improves on d0 by rolling back down to zero at the highest frequencies (u = ±0.5). However, it remains flat along the vertical v-axis, offering zero noise suppression in the perpendicular direction.

demo_steps.title: Roberts Cross: Diagonal bias
demo_steps.narration: The Roberts Cross operator takes differences diagonally. This rotates the frequency response by 45 degrees, creating a diagonal ridge. While compact, it is highly sensitive to directional high-frequency noise.

demo_steps.title: Sobel: The band-pass sweet spot
demo_steps.narration: The Sobel operator solves noise issues by multiplying the horizontal central difference by a vertical low-pass filter [1, 2, 1]. In 3D, this pinches the response into two localized band-pass lobes, suppressing high-frequency noise in both directions.

plan.camera_suggestion: Slightly elevated perspective showing all 4 subplots in a balanced grid. Rotating one plot rotates all 4 identically in real-time.
plan.view_reasoning: (1) In the source illustration, the u-axes run horizontally from left to right, and the v-axes recede into the page to the right. (2) This receding pattern implies a camera rotated roughly 35 degrees clockwise from a straight front-on view (azimuth around -35 degrees). (3) The surfaces are viewed from slightly above to reveal the peaks and valleys, implying a modest elevation angle of 22 degrees.

camera_view.view_notes: Synchronized camera ensures that dragging to orbit rotates all 4 subplots simultaneously, keeping their relative orientations perfectly aligned for direct visual comparison.

inline_constraints.explainers: clean tooltips on hovering over any point on the active surface displaying the exact (u, v) and DFT magnitude values
inline_constraints.framing: maintain ample margins around the 2x2 grid so that labels and axis arrows do not clip during rotation
inline_constraints.camera: must match the classic textbook isometric angle with synchronized multi-viewport rotations

plan.notes: Render the coordinate axes as extremely thin cylindrical lines (or simple custom lines with custom arrowheads) rather than thick 3D tubes to preserve the elegant look of the textbook drawing. Ensure the text labels of the vertical axes are explicitly translated outward relative to the camera to prevent clipping or overlap when the plot is rotated.

plan.contextChunk:
$$ {#eq-sobel_kernels}

This particular filter is called the **Sobel-Feldman operator**.  
The goal of this operator was to be compact and as isotropic as possible. The Sobel-Feldman operator can be implemented very efficiently as it can be written as the convolution with four small kernels, $$Sobel_x = b_1 \circ d_0 \circ b_1^T \circ b_1^T$$.

::: {.column-margin}
Remember that \(b_1=[1, 1]\), and \(b_2=b_1 \circ b_1 = [1,2,1]\).
:::

The DFT of the Sobel-Feldman operator is:

$$
Sobel_x \left[u,v \right] = D_1\left[u\right] B_2 \left[v \right] = j \sin \left( 2 \pi u /N \right) \left( 2+2 \cos \left(2 \pi v/N \right) \right)
$$

and it is shown in @fig-DFTderivativeoperators (d). \(N \times N\) is the extension of the domain (the operator is zero-padded). As \(d_0\) and \(d_1\) are 1D, their 2D DFT varies only along one dimension. The Roberts cross operator is similar to a rotated version of \(d_1\). The Sobel-Feldman operator has the profile of \(D_1\) along the axis \(u=0\) and it is proportional to the profile of \(B_2\) along any section \(v=\text{constant}\).

:::{#fig-DFTderivativeoperators}
![](figures/spatial_filters/DFTderivativeoperators.png){width=100%}

Magnitude of the DFT of four different discretizations of Gaussian derivatives: (a) \(d_0\); (b) \(d_1\); (c) Robert cross operator; and (d) Sobel-Feldman operator.
:::

@fig-DFTderivativeoperators compares the DFT of the four types of approximations of the derivatives that we have discussed. These operators are still very popular. \(Sobel\) has the best tolerance to noise due to its band-pass nature. The kernel \(d_0\) is the one that provides the highest resolution in the output. @fig-circle shows the output of different derivative approximations to a simple input image containing a circle. In the next section, we will discuss how to use these derivatives to extract other interesting quantities.

:::{#fig-circle}
![](figures/spatial_filters/circle.png){width=100%}

Derivatives of a circle along the directions $n$, $m$, and 45 degrees.  
The angle is shown only where the magnitude is $>0$. The derivative output along 45 degrees is obtained as a linear combination of the derivatives outputs along $n$ and $m$. Check the differences among the different kernels. The Sobel operator gives the most rotationally invariant gradient magnitude, but it is blurrier.
:::

## Image Gradient and Directional Derivatives

As we saw in @sec-simplesystem, an important image representation is given by the image gradient. From the image derivatives, we can also define the image gradient as the vector:
$$
\nabla \ell (x,y) = \left( \frac{\partial \ell(x,y)}{\partial x}, \frac{\partial \ell(x,y)}{\partial y} \right)
$$
For each pixel, the output is a 2D vector. In the case of using Gaussian derivatives, we can write:

elements: 3D coordinate axes with u-axis (horizontal/receding), v-axis (receding), and vertical magnitude axis with arrows
3D surface plot colored by height with a rainbow colormap (blue for zero, green/yellow for mid, red/yellow for peak)
Grid wireframe overlay on the surface to show the discretization and shape
Interactive hover probe displaying local coordinates (u, v) and DFT magnitude |H(u,v)|
Axis labels: 'u' (frequency along x), 'v' (frequency along y), and '|D(u,v)|' / '|H(u,v)|' on the vertical axis

interactions.label: Operator
interactions.teaches: Switches the 3D surface to compare different derivative filters, showing how their frequency-domain profiles shape their edge-detection properties

interactions.label: Show Grid Lines
interactions.teaches: Toggles the wireframe grid to clearly see the sampling density and surface curvature

demo_steps.title: d0: Simple Difference
demo_steps.narration: The d0 operator [1, -1] is a high-pass filter that computes differences between adjacent pixels. Its DFT magnitude rises monotonically toward the highest frequencies at u = ±0.5, but remains completely flat along the v-axis, making it highly sensitive to high-frequency noise and completely unshielded in the perpendicular direction.

demo_steps.title: d1: Central Difference
demo_steps.narration: The central difference d1 [1, 0, -1] acts over a wider span. In the frequency domain, its magnitude drops back to zero at the highest frequency boundary (u = ±0.5). While this reduces some extreme pixel-to-pixel noise compared to d0, the surface is still flat along the v-axis, meaning it has zero smoothing in the vertical direction.

demo_steps.title: Roberts Cross: Diagonal Edge Detection
demo_steps.narration: The Roberts Cross operator aligns its difference-taking along 45-degree diagonals. This rotates the entire frequency response, producing a diagonal pass-band ridge. It is extremely compact but highly sensitive to directional noise.

demo_steps.title: Sobel: Optimized Band-pass Lobe
demo_steps.narration: The Sobel-Feldman operator solves the noise problem by multiplying the central difference along u with a low-pass smoothing filter along v. This isolates the response into two smooth peaks (lobes) centered around the middle frequencies, suppressing noise at both the lowest and highest frequencies in both directions.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: Slightly elevated perspective looking from the front-left quadrant, so the u-axis extends rightwards, the v-axis recedes into the background, and the vertical magnitude peaks are fully visible without self-occlusion.
interactionPlan.view_reasoning: (1) In all four source subfigures, the u-axis runs from left to right across the bottom, while the v-axis recedes diagonally into the background to the right. (2) This receding baseline implies a camera position rotated to look from the front-left of the coordinate system, which corresponds to an azimuth around -35 degrees. (3) The vertical axes point straight up and the surfaces are viewed from slightly above, revealing the curved profile and saddle shapes, suggesting an elevation of 20-25 degrees.

camera_view.view_notes: The orthographic projection ensures the grid lines don't distort excessively, making the mathematical profiles of the different kernels directly comparable.

inline_constraints.default_view: matches the aspect ratio and clean white-background presentation of the original figure, replacing the static 4-panel grid with a single interactive, morphable 3D plot
inline_constraints.visible_ui: at most 2 compact toggles placed at the bottom edge to switch operators and grid visibility, keeping the view clean and centered on the geometry
inline_constraints.explainers: interactive tooltips when hovering over the surface to show the exact coordinates and magnitude values
inline_constraints.framing: keeps a moderate margin around the axes to prevent the labels from being cropped during rotation

interactionPlan.notes: Represent the surfaces mathematically: d0 = |sin(pi * u)|, d1 = |sin(2 * pi * u)|, Roberts = |sin(pi * (u + v))|, and Sobel = |sin(2 * pi * u) * cos^2(pi * v)|. Implement smooth vertex morphing when switching between 'operator' states so the user can visually trace how the flat profiles of d1 deform into the localized lobes of the Sobel filter.

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure displays four separate 3D surface plots arranged horizontally in a 1x4 layout. Each plot is viewed from a slightly elevated perspective looking down and left, with the 'u' axis extending to the right and the 'v' axis extending into the depth of the screen.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot shows the plots from a low, heavily foreshortened oblique angle, causing the panels to overlap extensively along the X/Z diagonal. The camera is zoomed in too closely, cutting off panels a) and d).
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The rendered view has a much flatter and more diagonal camera angle compared to the source, causing the four distinct panels to awkwardly overlap and crowd each other, whereas the source maintains clean, non-overlapping separation.
discrepancies: The four panels overlap extensively, whereas they should be spaced out horizontally with no overlap.
Panels a) and d) are mostly cut off from the view.
The labels (such as |R(u,v)| and |D1(u,v)|) are extremely oversized and collide with the 3D geometry.
The 'u' and 'v' axes are oriented differently than the original diagram.
evaluation.notes: The mathematical surface functions are represented correctly, but the layout is highly cluttered with severe overlaps and oversized labels.
action_items: Increase the horizontal spacing between the panels so they do not overlap.
Adjust the camera position and zoom to fit all four panels neatly within the viewport side-by-side.
Reduce the font size of all labels to prevent overlap and restore a clean textbook appearance.

Add interactive controls, such as a slider to rotate all surfaces simultaneously or buttons to focus on individual panels.
